items.q: Was the daguerreotype a negative-positive process?
items.correct: No — it produced a single unique image on a silvered plate; Talbot's calotype introduced the negative for multiple prints.
items.misconception: Yes — all early photography used negatives.
items.random_wrong: No — daguerreotypes were painted entirely by hand.
items.why: Students project the familiar negative-to-print workflow back onto the daguerreotype, which made one-off images.

items.q: What did Muybridge's motion studies of a galloping horse show?
items.correct: Sequential photographs proved that all four hooves leave the ground mid-gallop.
items.misconception: They proved a horse always keeps one hoof on the ground.
items.random_wrong: They proved cameras cannot capture motion.
items.why: Students invert the famous result; painters had imagined the gallop wrongly and Muybridge's sequence settled the debate.

items.q: Was photography immediately accepted as an art form?
items.correct: No — it was long dismissed as mechanical record, and movements like Pictorialism campaigned for its artistic status.
items.misconception: Yes — galleries embraced photography as art from 1839.
items.random_wrong: No — photography was banned from galleries until 1990.
items.why: Students assume photography's current status existed from the start, missing the long fight for its recognition as art.
The answer examiners want is: 1839. “1829” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Muybridge's galloping-horse study was made in:
items.why: It's 1878. “1868” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many cameras did Muybridge use to photograph the galloping horse in 1878?
items.why: The real figure is 12. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
